Did you know you can remotely control a target system without being detected by a firewall or many security systems?
Using a Reverse Shell over HTTP Cookies technique, you can send commands to a compromised system and receive the results in a highly covert manner, as if communicating through HTTP cookies.
How does the technique work?
The server (the attacker’s machine) sends a Base64-encoded command inside an HTTP cookie as part of an HTTP request.
The client (the target machine) receives the request, decodes the command, and executes it immediately on the system.
The command output is then encoded and sent back to the server inside another HTTP cookie, leaving little to no noticeable trace in the server logs.
Why is this technique notable?

• Stealth: The traffic appears as normal web browsing activity, making it less suspicious.

• * Firewall Evasion: The connection originates from the target machine to the server, which is generally considered normal outbound behavior.

• * Flexibility: It can be implemented in virtually any programming language (Python, PHP, and others).
Important Notice: This information is provided for educational and cybersecurity purposes only. Use these techniques only on systems you own or for which you have explicit written authorization.
